title: 12_debounce_identity.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Make the Debounce Actually Debounce

The search input fires an API call 300ms after the user stops typing. During a performance review the team noticed that rapid keystrokes fire one API call per character — exactly as if there were no debounce at all.

The root cause: `lodash.debounce` wraps a function and returns a new debounced function. That returned function maintains internal state (a timer, the pending call). The component is calling `debounce(search, 300)` during the render function itself, which creates a **new** debounced function on every render. Each render's debounced function has its own fresh timer with no pending calls. The user types, a render fires, a new debounced function is created, the timer starts fresh — and never completes before the next keystroke triggers another render and another new debounced function.

The debounced function must be created once and reused across renders so its internal timer state persists between keystrokes.
traceback:
FAIL  search-input.test.jsx > fires only one API call for rapid keystrokes within 300ms

  AssertionError: expected fetch to have been called 1 time, received 5 calls.

  User typed "react" (5 keystrokes within 200ms total).
  Expected: 1 debounced call after 300ms of silence.
  Received: 5 calls — one per keystroke — debounce had no effect.

  Root cause: a new debounced function is created on every render,
  discarding the previous timer on each keystroke.
correctOutput:
PASS  search-input.test.jsx > fires only one API call for rapid keystrokes within 300ms

  fetch called: 1 time (after 300ms of silence following the last keystroke)

options.label: Create the debounced function once with useRef so it persists across renders
options.resultLog:
PASS  search-input.test.jsx > fires only one API call for rapid keystrokes within 300ms

  fetch called: 1 time (after 300ms of silence following the last keystroke)
options.rationale: `useRef` creates a container whose `.current` property persists for the lifetime of the component instance. `useRef(debounce(...))` calls `debounce(...)` exactly once — on the first render — and stores the returned debounced function in `.current`. Every subsequent render accesses the same function object via `.current`, so the internal timer state is preserved across keystrokes. Appending `.current` immediately avoids wrapping the call in `ref.current(...)` at every call site.

options.label: Move the debounce call to module scope so it is created once when the file is imported
options.resultLog:
PASS  search-input.test.jsx > fires only one API call for rapid keystrokes within 300ms

  fetch called: 1 time ✓

  FAIL  search-input.test.jsx > two mounted SearchInput components debounce independently

  Both components share the same debounced function — typing in one delays the
  other. Module-scope state is shared across all instances.
options.rationale: Module-scope placement creates exactly one debounced function, which is stable across renders and tests pass. But module-scope variables are shared across *all instances* of the component on the page. If two `SearchInput` components are mounted simultaneously — in a split-view or a before/after comparison — they share the timer: typing in the first delays the second's pending call. Component-local state (via `useRef` or `useMemo`) scopes the debounced function to the component instance, which is the correct granularity.

options.label: Wrap the debounce call in useMemo with an empty dependency array
options.resultLog:
PASS  search-input.test.jsx > fires only one API call for rapid keystrokes within 300ms

  fetch called: 1 time ✓

  Note: The React docs say useMemo is not a *semantic* guarantee — React may discard
  the memoized value under memory pressure and recompute it. If recomputed, the timer
  resets mid-session. useRef is the documented stable-value pattern for this use case.
options.rationale: `useMemo` memoizes the result and returns the same value across renders as long as the dependencies are unchanged, which solves the immediate problem. The React documentation explicitly notes, however, that `useMemo` is an optimization hint — React may discard the cached value and recompute. For a debounced function, recomputation means a fresh timer and a burst of unthrottled requests. `useRef` is the documented pattern for storing an instance value that must survive all renders without exception.

hints: A debounced function works because it closes over a timer. Calling `debounce(fn, 300)` returns a new function with a fresh, empty timer each time. What React hook stores a value that is initialized once and then persists — unchanged — for the entire lifetime of the component instance?

Lines 13–15: `debounce(...)` is called inside the component body, so it runs on every render and creates a new debounced function with a blank timer. Store it in a `useRef` instead — `useRef(debounce(...)).current` initializes once on the first render and returns the same function object on every subsequent render.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`debounce(fn, delay)` returns a new function with internal state: a reference to the pending timer and the last arguments. That state is what makes debouncing work — successive calls within the delay window cancel and reschedule the timer. Calling `debounce(fn, delay)` inside the component body creates a *new* debounced function on every render. Each render produces a fresh timer with no pending call. The user types → render fires → new debounced function → fresh timer starts → next keystroke fires before 300ms → another render → another new debounced function → another fresh timer. The 300ms window never completes; every keystroke fires a call.

### Why the fix is correct
`useRef(initialValue)` creates a ref whose `.current` is set to `initialValue` on the first render and then *never changes* for the lifetime of the component instance, regardless of re-renders. `useRef(debounce(...)).current` initializes the debounced function once and reuses it forever. The internal timer state accumulates across keystrokes; when 300ms elapses without a new call, the pending invocation fires exactly once.
recruiterReview: Correct fix and the right tool. The `useMemo` distractor solves the immediate test but the React docs caveat — that `useMemo` is an optimization hint, not a stability guarantee — is real: React's concurrent mode can discard memoized values, and a reset timer mid-session produces the same burst-of-requests symptom in subtle ways. `useRef` is the documented pattern for component-instance values that must be stable. The module-scope distractor is the one that ships in code review when reviewers only check that the test passes — multi-instance shared state is a classic footgun that shows up when QA adds a comparison view. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Stable Instance Values in React: useRef vs. useMemo

React re-runs the component function on every render. Values created inside the function body — objects, functions, class instances — are recreated on every call. For most values this is fine; for values that maintain internal state across calls (debounced functions, throttled functions, timer IDs, third-party class instances) it is a bug.

### What debounce returns
`lodash.debounce(fn, delay)` returns a new function with internal state:
- A reference to the pending `setTimeout` timer
- The last set of arguments to call `fn` with
- A method to cancel the pending call (`.cancel()`)
- A method to invoke immediately (`.flush()`)

This state is what makes debouncing work. Calling `debounce(fn, delay)` again creates a new function with a blank timer — the previous pending call is lost. Creating it inside the component body means it is recreated on every render, discarding the timer each time.

### useRef: stable instance values
`useRef(initialValue)` is the React primitive for values that must:
- Be initialized once per component instance
- Persist across all renders
- Not trigger a re-render when they change

The pattern:
```jsx
const debouncedSearch = useRef(
  debounce(query => { /* ... */ }, 300)
).current;
```

Calling `.current` immediately provides the value (not the ref wrapper) at every render site, so the call site looks like `debouncedSearch(query)` rather than `debouncedSearch.current(query)`.

Other examples that belong in `useRef`:
- A `ResizeObserver` or `IntersectionObserver` instance
- A WebSocket connection
- A third-party chart/map library instance
- A `setInterval` timer ID (so cleanup can call `clearInterval`)

### useMemo: computed values, not stable instances
`useMemo(() => compute(), [deps])` memoizes a *computed value* and recomputes when deps change. React treats it as an optimization: it may discard the cached value and recompute during concurrent rendering. For a debounced function, recomputation resets the timer — the memoized function is no longer stable and debouncing breaks intermittently under concurrent renders. `useMemo` is the right tool for expensive derivations; `useRef` is the right tool for stable instance values.

### Module scope: shared across instances
Defining the debounced function at module scope creates one function shared by all component instances in the application. Two mounted `SearchInput` components share the timer; typing in one cancels the other's pending call. Module scope is correct for stateless utilities; for stateful instances, scope must match the component instance.

### Cleanup on unmount
Debounced functions should be cancelled when the component unmounts to prevent firing after the component is gone:
```jsx
const debouncedSearch = useRef(debounce(fn, 300));

useEffect(() => {
  return () => debouncedSearch.current.cancel();
}, []);
```

### Common variations of this bug
- A throttled function created inside the render body — same issue as debounce.
- `new IntersectionObserver(callback)` created inside render — a new observer is created on every render, never observing the target for long enough.
- `new Worker(url)` created inside render — a new worker thread per render, consuming memory until GC.
- A `ResizeObserver` created inside an event handler on each click rather than once per component.
- Any class instance whose methods depend on internal state (accumulated, queued, cached) being called repeatedly on the same instance.
